content:
# Exodus Chapter 18 -- The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~43v-44r (PDF pp. 82-83); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 59-60 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_018_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_018_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_018_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_018_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

> **MANUSCRIPT NOTE -- JETHRO'S VISIT AND THE ESTABLISHMENT OF JUDICIAL ADMINISTRATION:** The Leningrad Codex presents Exodus 18 in standard prose column format across two pages. A *petuchah* (open paragraph) marker appears before 18:1, marking the major transition from the Amalek battle and memorial (17:14-16) to the Jethro narrative -- a shift in setting, characters, and theme. A *setumah* (closed paragraph) before v. 5 separates the background information about Zipporah and the sons (vv. 2-4) from Jethro's arrival at the wilderness camp. Another *setumah* before v. 13 marks the transition from the worship meal (v. 12) to the judicial scene. A *petuchah* before v. 17 marks Jethro's counsel as a distinct unit. These divisions structure the chapter into four movements: reunion and arrival (vv. 1-7), Jethro's confession of YHWH's supremacy (vv. 8-12), the judicial problem (vv. 13-16), and Jethro's solution and its implementation (vv. 17-27). This chapter stands as a remarkable interlude between the wilderness journey narratives (chs. 15-17) and the Sinai theophany (chs. 19-20). It is the only chapter in the Torah where a non-Israelite -- Jethro, priest of Midian -- initiates a major theological confession and institutional reform within Israel. Jethro's confession in v. 11 -- "Now I know that YHWH is greater than all the gods" -- is one of the most significant declarations by a non-Israelite in the Hebrew Bible. The judicial reform in vv. 21-22 establishes the four-tier system of officials over thousands, hundreds, fifties, and tens that becomes foundational for Israel's governance. The chapter's placement before Sinai is deliberate: before Israel can receive the law, it must have a structure for administering it.

---

## Jethro Brings Moses' Family (vv. 1-6)

**18:1** And Jethro, priest of Midian, father-in-law of Moses, heard all that God had done for Moses and for Israel his people -- that YHWH had brought out Israel from Egypt. [fn1]

**18:2** And Jethro, father-in-law of Moses, took Zipporah, wife of Moses, after he had sent her away, [fn2]

**18:3** and her two sons, of whom the name of the one was Gershom -- for he said, "I have been a sojourner in a foreign land" -- [fn3]

**18:4** and the name of the other was Eliezer -- "for the God of my father was my help, and he delivered me from the sword of Pharaoh." [fn4]

**18:5** And Jethro, father-in-law of Moses, came -- and his sons and his wife -- to Moses, to the wilderness where he was encamped, at the mountain of God. [fn5]

**18:6** And he said to Moses, "I, your father-in-law Jethro, am coming to you, and your wife and her two sons with her." [fn6]

---

## Jethro's Confession of YHWH's Supremacy (vv. 7-12)

**18:7** And Moses went out to meet his father-in-law, and he bowed down and kissed him. And they asked each man of his companion concerning peace. And they came into the tent. [fn7]

**18:8** And Moses recounted to his father-in-law all that YHWH had done to Pharaoh and to Egypt on account of Israel -- all the hardship that had found them on the way -- and YHWH had delivered them. [fn8]

**18:9** And Jethro rejoiced over all the good that YHWH had done for Israel, that he had delivered them from the hand of Egypt. [fn9]

**18:10** And Jethro said, "Blessed be YHWH, who delivered you from the hand of Egypt and from the hand of Pharaoh, who delivered the people from under the hand of Egypt. [fn10]

**18:11** Now I know that YHWH is greater than all the gods -- indeed, in the very matter in which they acted arrogantly against them." [fn11]

**18:12** And Jethro, father-in-law of Moses, took a burnt offering and sacrifices for God. And Aaron came, and all the elders of Israel, to eat bread with the father-in-law of Moses before God. [fn12]

---

## The Burden of Solo Judgment (vv. 13-16)

**18:13** And it came to pass on the next day that Moses sat to judge the people. And the people stood over Moses from morning until evening. [fn13]

**18:14** And the father-in-law of Moses saw all that he was doing for the people, and he said, "What is this thing that you are doing for the people? Why do you sit alone, and all the people stand over you from morning until evening?" [fn14]

**18:15** And Moses said to his father-in-law, "Because the people come to me to inquire of God. [fn15]

**18:16** When they have a matter, it comes to me, and I judge between a man and his companion, and I make known the statutes of God and his instructions." [fn16]

---

## Jethro's Counsel: Delegation of Judgment (vv. 17-23)

**18:17** And the father-in-law of Moses said to him, "The thing that you are doing is not good. [fn17]

**18:18** Withering, you will wither -- both you and also this people who are with you -- for the thing is too heavy for you; you are not able to do it alone. [fn18]

**18:19** Now listen to my voice; I will counsel you, and may God be with you. You be for the people before God, and you shall bring the matters to God. [fn19]

**18:20** And you shall warn them concerning the statutes and the instructions, and you shall make known to them the way in which they must walk and the work that they must do. [fn20]

**18:21** And you shall look out from all the people men of ability, fearers of God, men of truth, haters of unjust gain. And you shall set over them officials over thousands, officials over hundreds, officials over fifties, and officials over tens. [fn21]

**18:22** And they shall judge the people at all times. And it shall be that every great matter they shall bring to you, and every small matter they shall judge themselves. So lighten it from upon yourself, and they shall bear it with you. [fn22]

**18:23** If you do this thing, and God commands you, then you will be able to endure, and also all this people shall go to their place in peace." [fn23]

---

## Moses Implements the Judicial Reform (vv. 24-27)

**18:24** And Moses listened to the voice of his father-in-law, and he did all that he had said. [fn24]

**18:25** And Moses chose men of ability from all Israel, and he set them as heads over the people -- officials over thousands, officials over hundreds, officials over fifties, and officials over tens. [fn25]

**18:26** And they judged the people at all times. The hard matter they would bring to Moses, and every small matter they would judge themselves. [fn26]

**18:27** And Moses sent his father-in-law away, and he went to his own land. [fn27]

---

> **[fn1] v.1 -- *Vayyishma Yitro kohen Midyan khoten Mosheh*: "And Jethro, priest of Midian, father-in-law of Moses, heard" -- a non-Israelite priest hears the exodus report** -- Hebrew: *vayyishma Yitro kohen Midyan khoten Mosheh et kol-asher asah Elohim le-Mosheh u-le-Yisra'el ammo ki-hotsi YHWH et-Yisra'el mi-Mitsrayim*. The name *Yitro* (Jethro) likely derives from the root *yatar* ("to exceed, to remain over"), suggesting "his excellence." He is also called *Re'u'el* ("friend of God," 2:18) and *Khovav* (Num. 10:29; Judg. 4:11) -- the multiple names likely reflect different traditions or different titles. The title *kohen Midyan* ("priest of Midian") identifies Jethro as a non-Israelite cultic functionary; the narrative treats his priesthood with respect throughout. The term *khoten* ("father-in-law," specifically the wife's father) appears an extraordinary thirteen times in this chapter, as though the narrator insistently frames the episode through kinship. The verse uses both divine names: *Elohim* ("God," the universal name) for the first clause and *YHWH* (the covenant name) for the second -- Jethro hears what Elohim did; the narrator specifies it was YHWH who brought Israel out. This dual naming may reflect the narrator's awareness that Jethro, as an outsider, would know God as *Elohim* rather than by the covenant name. EMET renders with extensive transliteration and bracket-glossing: "Yitro [Jethro], priest [kohen] of Midyan." LOGOS renders "Jethro, priest of Midian, father-in-law of Moses." PNEUMA renders "Jethro, the priest of Midian and Moses' father-in-law." The consensus follows LOGOS for formal English while preserving the transliterated divine name YHWH: "Jethro, priest of Midian, father-in-law of Moses." All three agree on rendering *vayyishma* as "heard" and *hotsi* as "brought out." LXX: *kai ekousen Iothor ho hiereus Madiam* -- uses *kurios* ("Lord") for both divine names, losing the Elohim/YHWH distinction; *gambros* ("father-in-law, kinsman by marriage") for *khoten*.

> **[fn2] v.2 -- *Akhar shillukheha*: "after he had sent her away" -- the ambiguity of Zipporah's departure** -- Hebrew: *vayyiqqakh Yitro khoten Mosheh et-Tsipporah eshet Mosheh akhar shillukheha*. The name *Tsipporah* (Zipporah) means "bird" (from *tsippor*). The critical phrase *akhar shillukheha* ("after her sending away") is theologically fraught. The noun *shillukhim* (from the Piel of *shalakh*, "to send away, to dismiss") can function as a technical term for divorce (cf. Deut. 24:1, 3 -- *sefer keritut*, "bill of divorce," in contexts using this same Piel form). The phrase therefore can mean either "after she was sent back" (a neutral dismissal for safety during the plagues and exodus) or "after her divorce" (a legal dissolution). The narrative provides no context for when or why this separation occurred; some traditions connect it to the circumcision crisis at 4:24-26, suggesting Aaron met Moses and counseled him to send Zipporah back before entering Egypt. What is clear is that during the entire exodus -- plagues, Passover, sea-crossing, wilderness -- Zipporah was absent. EMET renders "after her sending away [shillukheha]" with transliteration and extensive notes on the divorce ambiguity. LOGOS renders "after he had sent her away," preserving the ambiguity. PNEUMA renders "After Moses had sent her back," adding "Moses" as subject and choosing the neutral "sent back." The consensus renders "after he had sent her away" -- following LOGOS. This preserves the ambiguity of the Hebrew: the subject "he" is implied (Moses), and "sent away" captures the range of *shillukhim* without resolving whether this was a divorce or a temporary separation. PNEUMA's "sent back" is interpretive, tilting toward the neutral reading. The consensus leaves the question open, as the Hebrew does. LXX: *meta ten aphesin autes* ("after her release/dismissal") -- *aphesis* ("release, sending away") similarly preserves the ambiguity.

> **[fn3] vv.3-4 -- *Gershom...Eli'ezer*: the two sons as theological memoir** -- Hebrew: *ve-et shenei vaneha asher shem ha-ekhad Gershom ki amar ger hayiti be-erets nokhriyyah. Ve-shem ha-ekhad Eli'ezer ki Elohei avi be-ezri vayatssileni me-kherev Par'oh*. The name *Gershom* is a wordplay on *ger* ("sojourner") + *sham* ("there") -- "a sojourner there." The name was given at 2:22 and encapsulates Moses' Midianite exile. The noun *ger* ("sojourner, resident alien") denotes one who resides in a land not his own, without full rights of a native citizen. The name *Eli'ezer* ("my God is help," from *El* + *ezer*) appears here for the first time; his birth is not narrated in Exodus 2. The "sword of Pharaoh" (*kherev Par'oh*) refers to the death sentence Moses fled (2:15). The verb *vayatssileni* ("and he delivered me," Hiphil of *natsal*) is the same verb used for Israel's national deliverance throughout this chapter (vv. 8, 9, 10), creating a parallel between Moses' personal rescue and Israel's corporate exodus. EMET provides extensive transliteration and etymological notes. LOGOS renders "Gershom" and "Eliezer" without translation, with etymologies discussed in the notes. PNEUMA renders the names with translations embedded: "*Gershom* -- 'An Immigrant There'" and "*Eliezer* -- 'My God Is My Help.'" The consensus renders the names in standard English form (Gershom, Eliezer) without inline translation, following LOGOS, with the folk etymologies preserved in the surrounding text: "I have been a sojourner in a foreign land" and "the God of my father was my help." PNEUMA's embedded translations are vivid but depart from the formal register. LXX: the LXX reads "from the hand of Pharaoh" (*ek kheiros Pharao*) at v. 4 rather than "from the sword of Pharaoh" (*me-kherev Par'oh*) -- *kheir* ("hand") for *kherev* ("sword"), a notable variant. The consensus follows the MT.

> **[fn4] v.5 -- *Har ha-Elohim*: "the mountain of God" -- the site of the burning bush and the coming revelation** -- Hebrew: *vayyavo Yitro khoten Mosheh u-vanav ve-ishto el-Mosheh el-ha-midbar asher-hu khoneh sham har ha-Elohim*. The phrase *har ha-Elohim* ("the mountain of God") identifies the location as Horeb/Sinai (cf. 3:1; 4:27; 24:13) -- the very site of the burning bush and the coming covenant revelation. The participle *khoneh* (Qal of *khanah*, "to encamp") indicates an ongoing state: Moses was camping there, not merely passing through. The "sons" (*vanav*) and "wife" (*ishto*) are Moses' family members, brought by Jethro. All three translators agree on "the mountain of God" for *har ha-Elohim*. EMET provides heavy transliteration: "the midbar [wilderness]...har [mountain] of ha-Elohim [God]." LOGOS renders "the mountain of God." PNEUMA renders "near the mountain of God." The consensus renders "at the mountain of God" -- following LOGOS for the formal phrase; "at" rather than PNEUMA's "near" follows the Hebrew locative more closely. LXX: *epi orous tou theou* ("at the mountain of God") -- preserving the Hebrew.

> **[fn5] v.6 -- The announcement of arrival: first-person self-identification or third-person report** -- Hebrew: *vayyomer el-Mosheh ani khotenkha Yitro ba elekha ve-ishtekha u-shenei vaneha immah*. This verse likely represents a message sent ahead by messenger, since v. 7 describes Moses going out to meet Jethro -- he would not go out to meet someone already before him. The self-identification formula "I, your father-in-law Jethro" is formal. The participle *ba* ("coming," from *bo*) indicates imminent arrival. The phrase "her two sons with her" (*u-shenei vaneha immah*) emphasizes that the sons are in their mother's care. All three translators agree on substance. The consensus follows LOGOS: "I, your father-in-law Jethro, am coming to you, and your wife and her two sons with her." LXX: the LXX changes the first-person announcement to a third-person report: *anengelei Mousei legontes* ("it was announced to Moses, saying") -- making explicit the messenger intermediary that the Hebrew only implies. The LXX also reads "your two sons" (*hoi duo huioi sou*) rather than "her two sons" (*shenei vaneha*) -- shifting from maternal to paternal connection. The consensus follows the MT.

> **[fn6] v.7 -- *Vayyishtakhu vayyishshaq-lo vayyish'alu ish-le-re'ehu le-shalom*: the formal Near Eastern greeting** -- Hebrew: *vayyetse Mosheh liqrat khotno vayyishtakhu vayyishshaq-lo vayyish'alu ish-le-re'ehu le-shalom vayyavo'u ha-ohelah*. The sequence of verbs paints a formal greeting: Moses *goes out* to meet his father-in-law (a mark of honor), *bows down* (Hishtaphel of *shakhah*, the standard verb for obeisance/worship -- the leader of Israel bows to the Midianite priest), *kisses him* (the kinsman's greeting, cf. Gen. 29:13; 33:4), and they *ask each man of his companion concerning shalom* (reciprocal well-being inquiry). The verb *vayish'alu...le-shalom* ("asked concerning peace") uses *shalom* in its fullest sense -- not merely peace but wholeness, completeness, well-being. The move into the tent (*ha-ohelah*, with directional *he*) transitions from public greeting to private conversation. EMET renders with full transliteration: "bowed down [vayishtakhu] and he kissed him [vayishshaq-lo]." LOGOS renders "he bowed down and he kissed him." PNEUMA renders "bowed down before him, and kissed him." The consensus renders "he bowed down and kissed him" -- following LOGOS for formal English, without EMET's bracketing or PNEUMA's added "before him" (which is implied but not in the Hebrew). For the greeting, "asked each man of his companion concerning peace" preserves the reciprocal idiom *ish-le-re'ehu le-shalom*. LXX: *prosekunesen auto kai ephilesen auton* ("bowed down to him and kissed him") -- *proskuneo* ("to prostrate, to worship") for *vayyishtakhu*.

> **[fn7] v.8 -- *Ha-tela'ah*: "the hardship" -- Moses recounts the full exodus narrative** -- Hebrew: *vayesapper Mosheh le-khotno et kol-asher asah YHWH le-Par'oh u-le-Mitsrayim al odot Yisra'el et kol-ha-tela'ah asher metsa'atam ba-derekh vayatssilem YHWH*. The verb *vayesapper* (Piel of *safar*, "to recount, narrate") indicates a thorough, detailed retelling. The noun *tela'ah* ("hardship, weariness, distress," from *la'ah*, "to be weary") is relatively rare (cf. Num. 20:14; Neh. 9:32; Lam. 3:5) and captures the accumulated, grinding difficulty of the journey. The phrase *metsa'atam* ("that found them") presents hardship as an external force that overtakes the people. The structure is tripartite: (1) what YHWH did to Pharaoh and Egypt, (2) all the hardship that found them on the road, (3) how YHWH delivered them. EMET renders "recounted [vayesapper]...hardship [ha-tela'ah]." LOGOS renders "recounted...hardship." PNEUMA renders "told...the whole story...all the hardships." All three agree on "hardship" for *tela'ah*. The consensus renders "recounted" (EMET/LOGOS) rather than "told" (PNEUMA) -- *vayesapper* in the Piel indicates detailed narration, which "recounted" better captures. LXX: the LXX expands the ending with "from the hand of Pharaoh and from the hand of the Egyptians" -- not in the MT. The consensus follows the MT.

> **[fn8] v.9 -- *Vayyikhad*: "rejoiced" -- the hapax legomenon and its ambiguity** -- Hebrew: *vayyikhad Yitro al kol-ha-tovah asher asah YHWH le-Yisra'el asher hittsilo mi-yad Mitsrayim*. The verb *vayyikhad* is a hapax legomenon -- it occurs only here in the Hebrew Bible. Its derivation is disputed. The primary interpretation derives it from *khadah* ("to rejoice, to be glad," cognate with Aramaic *khadi*), yielding "Jethro rejoiced." An alternative interpretation, found in the Talmud (Sanhedrin 94a), derives it from *khad* ("sharp") -- "his skin became prickly" or "goosebumps came upon his flesh" -- suggesting mixed emotions: joy for Israel but distress for Egypt's destruction, since Jethro was himself a non-Israelite. The Midrash (Mekhilta) records both: Rabbi Yehoshua says "he rejoiced"; Rabbi Elazar HaModa'i says "his flesh got goose-bumps." EMET renders "rejoiced [vayikhad]" with extensive notes on the ambiguity. LOGOS renders "rejoiced," following the primary lexical tradition. PNEUMA renders "was overjoyed," intensifying. The consensus renders "rejoiced" -- following EMET/LOGOS. "Rejoiced" captures the primary lexical meaning of the hapax without resolving the Talmudic ambiguity, which is noted here. PNEUMA's "overjoyed" overstates the certainty of interpretation given the textual debate. LXX: *exeste* ("he was amazed, astonished, beside himself") -- reading neither joy nor discomfort but overwhelming astonishment, a third interpretive possibility. The consensus follows the majority Hebrew lexical tradition.

> **[fn9] v.10 -- *Barukh YHWH*: "Blessed be YHWH" -- a Midianite priest's blessing** -- Hebrew: *vayyomer Yitro barukh YHWH asher hittsil etkhem mi-yad Mitsrayim u-mi-yad Par'oh asher hittsil et-ha-am mi-takhat yad-Mitsrayim*. The *berakhah* formula *barukh YHWH* ("blessed be YHWH") is remarkable coming from a non-Israelite priest. *Barukh* is the Qal passive participle of *barakh* ("to bless, to kneel"). This parallels Melchizedek's blessing in Gen. 14:19-20. The double use of *hittsil* ("delivered," Hiphil of *natsal*) creates emphasis: YHWH delivered both "you" (the leaders, plural) and "the people" as a whole. The phrase *mi-takhat yad* ("from under the hand of") creates a vivid image: Israel trapped beneath the crushing hand of Egypt, from which YHWH pulled them free. The triple use of *yad* ("hand") in this verse -- "from the hand of Egypt," "from the hand of Pharaoh," "from under the hand of Egypt" -- hammers the image of oppressive power overcome by divine rescue. All three translators agree on substance. The consensus follows LOGOS for the formal rendering: "Blessed be YHWH, who delivered you from the hand of Egypt and from the hand of Pharaoh, who delivered the people from under the hand of Egypt." LXX: *eulogetos kurios* ("blessed be the Lord") for *barukh YHWH* -- the standard Greek rendering.

> **[fn10] v.11 -- *Attah yadati ki-gadol YHWH mi-kol-ha-elohim*: "Now I know that YHWH is greater than all the gods" -- the theological climax** -- Hebrew: *attah yadati ki-gadol YHWH mi-kol-ha-elohim ki va-davar asher zadu aleihem*. This is Jethro's confession -- the theological climax of the chapter. The adverb *attah* ("now") marks a turning point: there was a before, when Jethro did not know or did not fully know, and there is a now, when knowledge has become certain. The verb *yadati* ("I know," Qal perfect of *yada'*) denotes experiential, convicted knowledge -- not abstract belief but the settled certainty that comes from weighing evidence. The comparative *ki-gadol YHWH mi-kol-ha-elohim* ("that YHWH is great above all the gods") uses the preposition *min* with *kol* + *ha-elohim* to assert YHWH's supremacy. This is not strict monotheism (denying the existence of other gods) but henotheism or monolatry -- the declaration of YHWH's supremacy among the gods. This is precisely the kind of claim the exodus was designed to demonstrate (cf. 12:12 -- "against all the gods of Egypt I will execute judgments"). The second clause is notoriously obscure: *ki va-davar asher zadu aleihem* ("indeed, in the matter in which they acted arrogantly against them"). The verb *zadu* (Qal of *zud/zid*, "to act presumptuously, to boil over with arrogance") describes insolent overreaching. The subject "they" and the object "them" are both ambiguous. The dominant reading (Talmud, Sotah 11a; Rashi) sees measure-for-measure justice (*middah ke-neged middah*): the Egyptians drowned Israel's sons in the Nile, and YHWH drowned the Egyptians in the sea. The sentence is grammatically incomplete -- it trails off, as if Jethro is overwhelmed by the realization. EMET renders "great [gadol] above all [mi-kol] the gods [ha-elohim]; indeed [ki], in the thing [ba-davar] in which they acted arrogantly [zadu] upon them [aleihem]." LOGOS renders "the LORD is greater than all the gods, indeed, in the matter in which they acted presumptuously against them." PNEUMA renders "YHWH is greater than all gods -- for in the very thing they plotted arrogantly, he was above them." The consensus renders "YHWH is greater than all the gods -- indeed, in the very matter in which they acted arrogantly against them" -- blending LOGOS's formal structure with PNEUMA's "the very matter" (which captures the emphatic *ba-davar*). "Arrogantly" (EMET/PNEUMA) is preferred over "presumptuously" (LOGOS) as a more accessible rendering of *zadu*. The sentence is left grammatically incomplete, as in the Hebrew. LXX: *hoti megas kurios para pantas tous theous* ("because the Lord is great beyond all the gods"); *dioti epethento autois* ("because they set upon them") -- rendering *zadu* as *epitithemi* ("to attack") rather than "to act arrogantly," a significant interpretive shift. The consensus follows the MT.

> **[fn11] v.12 -- *Olah u-zevakhim le-Elohim...le-ekhol-lekhem...lifnei ha-Elohim*: a Midianite priest sacrifices to the God of Israel** -- Hebrew: *vayyiqqakh Yitro khoten Mosheh olah u-zevakhim le-Elohim vayyavo Aharon ve-khol ziqnei Yisra'el le-ekhol-lekhem im-khoten Mosheh lifnei ha-Elohim*. This verse is theologically extraordinary: a Midianite priest offers sacrifices to the God of Israel, and Aaron and the elders participate in a communal meal "before God." The *olah* ("burnt offering," from *alah*, "to go up") is entirely consumed on the altar -- total dedication to God. The *zevakhim* ("sacrifices," from *zavakh*, "to slaughter") are communion offerings whose meat is shared in a sacred meal -- this explains the eating in v. 12b. The phrase *le-Elohim* ("for God") uses the universal divine name rather than *la-YHWH*, perhaps reflecting Jethro's still-developing theological vocabulary. The phrase *le-ekhol-lekhem* ("to eat bread/food") describes the communal sacrificial meal; *lekhem* here means "food" broadly. That this occurs *lifnei ha-Elohim* ("before God") marks it as a sacred, covenantal act in the divine presence. Moses' conspicuous absence from the meal is noted by many commentators; the text mentions only Aaron and the elders. EMET renders with full transliteration: "an olah [burnt-offering] and zevakhim [sacrifices]...to eat lekhem [bread/food]." LOGOS renders "a burnt offering and sacrifices for God...to eat bread...before God." PNEUMA renders "a burnt offering and sacrifices to God...to share a meal...in the presence of God." The consensus renders "a burnt offering and sacrifices for God...to eat bread...before God" -- following LOGOS for the formal rendering. "Burnt offering" is preferred over EMET's transliterated *olah* in the running text. "To eat bread" (LOGOS) rather than "to share a meal" (PNEUMA) preserves the Hebrew idiom *le-ekhol-lekhem*. "Before God" (LOGOS) rather than "in the presence of God" (PNEUMA) renders *lifnei ha-Elohim* more literally. LXX: *holokautomata kai thusias to theo* ("whole-burnt-offerings and sacrifices for God") -- *holokautoma* for *olah*; *thusia* for *zevakh*.

> **[fn12] v.13 -- *Vayyeshev Mosheh lishpot et-ha-am*: "Moses sat to judge the people" -- the judicial posture** -- Hebrew: *vayehi mi-makharat vayyeshev Mosheh lishpot et-ha-am vayyya'amod ha-am al-Mosheh min-ha-boqer ad-ha-arev*. The judge sits; the litigants stand. *Vayyeshev* (Qal of *yashav*, "to sit") is the posture of judicial authority (cf. Ps. 122:5 -- "there sat the thrones of judgment"). The verb *lishpot* (Qal infinitive of *shafat*, "to judge, govern, decide disputes") encompasses the full range of judicial activity. The people stand *al-Mosheh* ("over Moses," the preposition *al* suggesting they pressed around him, crowding in). The time frame *min-ha-boqer ad-ha-arev* ("from the morning until the evening") indicates an all-day, every-day burden. All three translators agree on substance. The consensus follows LOGOS for "it came to pass on the next day" rather than PNEUMA's "the next day" -- preserving the Hebrew *vayehi* narrative formula. "Stood over Moses" follows the MT *al-Mosheh*. LXX: *sunekathisen Mouses krinein ton laon* ("Moses sat down to judge the people") -- *krino* ("to judge") for *shafat*.

> **[fn13] v.14 -- *Levaddekha*: "alone" -- the diagnosis of unsustainable leadership** -- Hebrew: *vayyar khoten Mosheh et kol-asher hu oseh la-am vayyomer mah ha-davar ha-zeh asher attah oseh la-am maddua attah yoshev levaddekha ve-khol-ha-am nitsav alekha min-boqer ad-arev*. The key word is *levaddekha* ("alone, by yourself," from *bad*, "alone, separate" + 2ms suffix). Jethro sees dysfunction immediately -- his question is not merely informational but diagnostic. The participle *nitsav* (Niphal of *yatsav*, "to take one's stand, station oneself") describes the people standing, stationed, immobile, waiting. All three translators agree on the rendering. The consensus follows LOGOS for formal English: "Why do you sit alone, and all the people stand over you from morning until evening?" LXX: *dia ti su kathesai monos* ("why do you sit alone") -- *monos* ("alone") for *levaddekha*.

> **[fn14] vv.15-16 -- *Lidrosh Elohim...khuqqei ha-Elohim ve-et-torotav*: Moses' twofold function as oracle and teacher** -- Hebrew: *ki-yavo elai ha-am lidrosh Elohim* (v. 15); *ki-yihyeh lahem davar ba elai ve-shafatti bein ish u-vein re'ehu ve-hodati et-khuqqei ha-Elohim ve-et-torotav* (v. 16). Moses' role is twofold: (1) *lidrosh Elohim* ("to inquire of God," from *darash*, "to seek, inquire, investigate, study") -- the people come to Moses as a prophetic oracle, seeking divine guidance; and (2) *ve-hodati et-khuqqei ha-Elohim ve-et-torotav* ("I make known the statutes of God and his instructions") -- Moses teaches divine law. The noun *khuqqim* ("statutes," from *khaqaq*, "to engrave, inscribe") denotes fixed, inscribed regulations. The noun *torot* (plural of *torah*, "instruction, teaching, law," from *yarah*, "to direct, teach") is notable in the plural -- multiple divine directives existing before Sinai. The combination *khuqqim* + *torot* anticipates the fuller legal vocabulary of the Sinai revelation. EMET renders "to inquire [lidrosh] of Elohim [God]...statutes [khuqqei]...his torot [instructions/laws]." LOGOS renders "to inquire of God...statutes of God and His laws." PNEUMA renders "to seek God's guidance...God's statutes and instructions." The consensus renders "to inquire of God" (EMET/LOGOS) rather than "to seek God's guidance" (PNEUMA) -- preserving the oracular connotation of *darash*. For *torotav*, the consensus renders "his instructions" rather than LOGOS's "His laws" or EMET's "his torot" -- *torah* in the plural fundamentally means "instructions, teachings, directives" rather than "laws" in the modern statutory sense. LXX: *ekzetesai krisin para tou theou* ("to seek judgment from God") -- adding *krisis* ("judgment") where the Hebrew simply says "to inquire of God."

> **[fn15] v.17 -- *Lo-tov*: "not good" -- the Genesis 2:18 echo** -- Hebrew: *vayyomer khoten Mosheh elav lo-tov ha-davar asher attah oseh*. The phrase *lo-tov* ("not good") deliberately echoes Genesis 2:18 -- *lo-tov heyot ha-adam levaddo* ("it is not good for the man to be alone"). Just as it was "not good" for Adam to be alone and God provided a helper, so it is "not good" for Moses to judge alone and Jethro provides a system. The structural parallel is deliberate: the solution in both cases is the provision of helpers; the solution to aloneness is community; the solution to overwork is delegation. Jethro speaks with the authority of a father-in-law and the directness of an experienced leader. All three translators render "not good" for *lo-tov*. The consensus follows LOGOS: "The thing that you are doing is not good." LXX: *ouk orthos su poieis to rhema touto* ("you are not doing this thing rightly") -- *orthos* ("rightly, correctly") shifts from "not good" (a quality of the thing) to "not rightly" (a manner of the action). The consensus follows the MT.

> **[fn16] v.18 -- *Navol tibbol*: "withering, you will wither" -- the infinitive absolute of collapse** -- Hebrew: *navol tibbol gam-attah gam ha-am ha-zeh asher immakh ki-kaved mimmekha ha-davar lo-tukhal asotho levaddekha*. The infinitive absolute construction *navol tibbol* (infinitive absolute + imperfect of *navel*, "to wither, fade, wear out") creates emphatic certainty: "you will surely wither." The root *navel* is the same as *naval* ("fool, senseless person," cf. 1 Sam. 25:25) and *nevelah* ("carcass, corpse"). The progression is from vitality to withering to death. The double *gam...gam* ("both...and also") emphasizes that both Moses and the people suffer. The keyword *kaved* ("heavy") shares its root with *kavod* ("glory") and echoes 17:12 where Moses' hands were "heavy" (*kvedim*). EMET renders "Wearing away you will wear away [navol tibbol]." LOGOS renders "You will surely wear out." PNEUMA renders "You will surely wear yourself out." The consensus renders "Withering, you will wither" -- a rendering that preserves both the infinitive absolute construction (showing the two-word Hebrew form) and the organic imagery of the root *navel* (which denotes withering like a leaf, not merely "wearing out"). "Withering, you will wither" is more faithful to the botanical metaphor than LOGOS/PNEUMA's "wear out" while remaining English-accessible. EMET's "wearing away" also captures the construction but loses the plant imagery. LXX: *phthora kataphthareise* ("with corruption you will be utterly corrupted") -- *phthora* ("corruption, destruction") for *navol tibbol*, intensifying toward destruction rather than withering.

> **[fn17] vv.19-20 -- *Mul ha-Elohim...ve-hizhartah*: Moses' retained functions** -- Hebrew: *attah shema be-qoli i'atskha vihi Elohim immakh heyeh attah la-am mul ha-Elohim ve-heveita attah et-ha-devarim el-ha-Elohim* (v. 19); *ve-hizhartah ethem et-ha-khuqqim ve-et-ha-torot ve-hodata lahem et-ha-derekh yelkhu vah ve-et-ha-ma'aseh asher ya'asun* (v. 20). Jethro's reform begins by preserving Moses' irreplaceable functions: (1) *heyeh attah la-am mul ha-Elohim* ("you be for the people before God") -- the mediator standing *mul* ("before, opposite, facing") God on the people's behalf; (2) *ve-heveita...et-ha-devarim el-ha-Elohim* ("bring the matters to God") -- carrying cases into the divine presence; (3) *ve-hizhartah* ("warn, enlighten," Hiphil of *zahar*, "to shine, warn") -- proactively teaching the statutes and instructions; (4) *ve-hodata lahem et-ha-derekh...ve-et-ha-ma'aseh* ("make known the way...and the work") -- showing the path of faithful living. Only routine adjudication is delegated. The verb *i'atskha* ("I will counsel you," from *ya'ats*) is the root of *yo'ets* ("counselor"), one of the messianic titles in Isa. 9:5. All three translators agree on substance. The consensus follows LOGOS for formal English: "You be for the people before God, and you shall bring the matters to God...you shall warn them concerning the statutes and the instructions, and you shall make known to them the way in which they must walk and the work that they must do." PNEUMA's "Teach them" for *ve-hizhartah* loses the warning dimension of the Hiphil of *zahar*. LXX: *diamarturai autois* ("you shall testify solemnly to them") for *ve-hizhartah*.

> **[fn18] v.21 -- *Anshei-khayil yir'ei Elohim anshei emet son'ei vatsa*: the four qualifications and the four-tier system** -- Hebrew: *ve-attah tekhezeh mi-kol-ha-am anshei-khayil yir'ei Elohim anshei emet son'ei vatsa ve-samta aleihem sarei alafim sarei me'ot sarei khamishim ve-sarei asarot*. The verb *tekhezeh* (Qal of *khazah*, "to see, perceive, envision") implies prophetic discernment -- stronger than ordinary *ra'ah* ("to see"). Moses must see with divine insight who is fit to judge. **The four qualifications:** (1) *anshei-khayil* ("men of ability/valor") -- *khayil* encompasses military valor, moral strength, administrative competence, and resourcefulness. The same word appears in *eshet-khayil* ("woman of valor," Prov. 31:10). (2) *yir'ei Elohim* ("fearers of God") -- those who possess *yir'ah* ("fear, reverence, awe"), the foundational disposition of wisdom (Prov. 1:7; 9:10) and the safeguard against perverting justice for human advantage. (3) *anshei emet* ("men of truth") -- *emet* ("truth, reliability, faithfulness," from *aman*, "to be firm") denotes people whose word corresponds to reality. (4) *son'ei vatsa* ("haters of unjust gain") -- *betsa* ("profit, unjust gain," from *batsa'*, "to cut off, gain by violence") denotes the gain taken by corruption; a judge who hates *betsa* cannot be bribed. These four criteria move from general competence to specific ethical commitments: capability, piety, integrity, incorruptibility. **The four-tier system:** *sarei alafim, sarei me'ot, sarei khamishim, ve-sarei asarot* ("officials over thousands, hundreds, fifties, and tens") -- a decimal hierarchy creating access to justice at every level. The noun *sar* ("official, prince, commander," from *sarar*, "to rule") is flexible for both military and administrative contexts. EMET renders "men of khayil [valor/ability]" with transliteration and "commanders of thousands/hundreds/fifties/tens." LOGOS renders "men of ability" and "rulers of thousands/hundreds/fifties/tens." PNEUMA renders "capable men" and "officials over groups of thousands, hundreds, fifties, and tens." The consensus renders "men of ability" (LOGOS) for *anshei-khayil* -- "ability" captures the breadth of *khayil* better than PNEUMA's "capable men" while being more accessible than EMET's transliterated *khayil*. "Fearers of God" (EMET/LOGOS) for *yir'ei Elohim* -- preserving the Hebrew participial construction. "Men of truth" (EMET/LOGOS) for *anshei emet* -- PNEUMA's "men of integrity" is a valid dynamic rendering but *emet* specifically means "truth, faithfulness," not the broader "integrity." "Haters of unjust gain" (EMET/LOGOS) for *son'ei vatsa* -- PNEUMA's "men who despise dishonest profit" is accurate but wordy. For the hierarchy, the consensus renders "officials" -- a compromise between EMET's "commanders" (too military), LOGOS's "rulers" (too monarchic), and PNEUMA's "officials" (appropriately administrative). LXX: *andras dunatous* ("capable men") for *anshei-khayil*; *theosebeis* ("God-fearing") for *yir'ei Elohim*; *andras dikaious* ("righteous men") for *anshei emet* -- rendering "truth" as "righteousness," a significant shift; *misountas huperephanian* ("hating arrogance") for *son'ei vatsa* -- rendering "unjust gain" as "arrogance," another significant shift. The consensus follows the MT.

> **[fn19] v.22 -- *Ha-davar ha-gadol...ha-davar ha-qaton*: "the great matter...the small matter" -- the principle of judicial hierarchy** -- Hebrew: *ve-shaftu et-ha-am be-khol-et ve-hayah kol-ha-davar ha-gadol yavi'u elekha ve-khol-ha-davar ha-qaton yishpetu hem ve-haqel me-alekha ve-nas'u ittakh*. The distinction between *davar gadol* ("great matter") and *davar qaton* ("small matter") establishes the principle of appellate jurisdiction: complex or weighty cases ascend to Moses; routine disputes are settled locally. The imperative *ve-haqel* (Hiphil of *qalal*, "to be light") -- "lighten!" -- is the opposite of *kaved* ("heavy") from v. 18. The verb *ve-nas'u* (Qal of *nasa*, "to lift, carry, bear") -- "they shall bear" -- describes shared burden: not instead of Moses but alongside him (*ittakh*, "with you"). EMET renders "every great [ha-gadol] matter...every small [ha-qaton] matter." LOGOS renders "every great matter...every small matter." PNEUMA renders "every major case...every minor case." The consensus follows LOGOS: "every great matter they shall bring to you, and every small matter they shall judge themselves." PNEUMA's "major case...minor case" is valid but loses the Hebrew adjectives *gadol/qaton*. For *ve-haqel*, the consensus renders "lighten it from upon yourself" -- preserving the imperative force. LXX: *to rhema to huperogkon* ("the weighty matter") for *ha-davar ha-gadol*; *ta brachea ton krimaton* ("the small judgments") for *ha-davar ha-qaton*.

> **[fn20] v.23 -- *Ve-tsivvekha Elohim*: "and God commands you" -- human wisdom conditioned by divine sanction** -- Hebrew: *im et-ha-davar ha-zeh ta'aseh ve-tsivvekha Elohim ve-yakholta amod ve-gam kol-ha-am ha-zeh al-meqomo yavo ve-shalom*. The clause *ve-tsivvekha Elohim* ("and God commands you," Piel of *tsavah*, "to command") is a crucial qualification: Jethro's advice is conditional upon divine approval. Despite his authority and wisdom, Jethro does not presume to override YHWH. The promise is twofold: (1) *ve-yakholta amod* ("you will be able to stand/endure") -- Moses will survive; (2) *al-meqomo yavo ve-shalom* ("to their place they shall come in peace") -- the people will go home satisfied, with disputes resolved. *Shalom* here means more than absence of conflict -- it is the satisfaction of justice received. EMET renders "and Elohim [God] commands you [ve-tsivvekha]...upon their place [al-meqomo] they shall come [yavo'u] in shalom [peace]." LOGOS renders "and God commands you...you will be able to endure...shall go to its place in peace." PNEUMA renders "and God so commands you...you will be able to endure, and all these people too will go home satisfied." The consensus renders "and God commands you, then you will be able to endure, and also all this people shall go to their place in peace" -- following LOGOS for formal structure while rendering *amod* as "endure" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) rather than EMET's literal "stand." PNEUMA's "go home satisfied" interprets *al-meqomo...ve-shalom* dynamically; the consensus preserves the literal "to their place in peace." LXX: *katischusei se ho theos* ("God will strengthen you") for *ve-tsivvekha Elohim* -- rendering "commands" as "strengthens," a significant shift from divine sanction to divine empowerment. The consensus follows the MT.

> **[fn21] vv.24-25 -- *Vayyishma Mosheh le-qol khotno*: "Moses listened to the voice of his father-in-law" -- obedience to wisdom** -- Hebrew: *vayyishma Mosheh le-qol khotno vayyya'as kol asher amar* (v. 24); *vayyivkhar Mosheh anshei-khayil mi-kol-Yisra'el vayyitten otam rashim al-ha-am sarei alafim sarei me'ot sarei khamishim ve-sarei asarot* (v. 25). The construction *vayyishma...le-qol* ("listened to the voice of") indicates obedient heeding -- the same construction used for obedience to YHWH's voice (cf. Gen. 22:18; Exod. 15:26). Moses, who speaks with YHWH face to face, humbly accepts counsel from his Midianite father-in-law. The verb *vayyivkhar* (Qal of *bakhar*, "to choose, select") -- the same verb Moses used for Joshua in 17:9. The officers are called *rashim* ("heads," from *rosh*). Note that only the first qualification (*anshei-khayil*) is mentioned in the implementation -- the text does not repeat all four from v. 21, which may be narrative economy or may suggest the full ideal was not perfectly achieved. All three translators agree on substance. The consensus renders "Moses listened to the voice of his father-in-law, and he did all that he had said" -- following LOGOS for formal English. For v. 25: "men of ability" and "heads over the people" for *rashim al-ha-am*. LXX: *ekousen Mouses tes phones tou gambrou* ("Moses hearkened to the voice of his father-in-law").

> **[fn22] v.26 -- *Ha-davar ha-qashah*: "the hard matter" -- the shift from *gadol* to *qashah*** -- Hebrew: *ve-shaftu et-ha-am be-khol-et et-ha-davar ha-qashah yevi'un el-Mosheh ve-khol-ha-davar ha-qaton yishputu hem*. A significant shift occurs between v. 22 (Jethro's proposal) and v. 26 (the implementation): Jethro said the *gadol* ("great/large") matters would go to Moses, but in practice it is the *qashah* ("hard/difficult") matters that are escalated. The adjective *qashah* ("hard, difficult, severe," from the same root as Pharaoh's "hard" heart, 7:3) shifts the criterion from scale to difficulty. It is not the social importance of the disputants but the legal complexity of the case that triggers escalation. The verb *yevi'un* (Hiphil with paragogic *nun*) gives an archaic or emphatic flavor. EMET renders "the hard [ha-qashah] matter." LOGOS renders "the hard matter." PNEUMA renders "the difficult cases." The consensus renders "the hard matter" (EMET/LOGOS) -- preserving the Hebrew adjective *qashah*. The shift from *gadol* (v. 22) to *qashah* (v. 26) is preserved without harmonization, following the MT. LXX: *pan rhema huperogkon* ("every weighty matter") for *ha-davar ha-qashah* -- using *huperogkos* ("weighty") for both *gadol* (v. 22) and *qashah* (v. 26), losing the Hebrew distinction.

> **[fn23] v.27 -- *Vayyeshallakh Mosheh et-khotno vayyeylekh lo el-artso*: "Moses sent his father-in-law away, and he went to his own land" -- the departure** -- Hebrew: *vayyeshallakh Mosheh et-khotno vayyeylekh lo el-artso*. The verb *vayyeshallakh* (Piel of *shalakh*, "to send away") echoes the *shillukheha* ("her sending away") of v. 2 -- the same root governs both the earlier departure of Zipporah and the final departure of Jethro. But where Zipporah's sending was ambiguous, Jethro's is an honorable farewell. The ethical dative *lo* ("for himself") suggests voluntary, purposeful departure. The destination *el-artso* ("to his land") is Midian -- Jethro returns to his own people, his own priesthood, his own land. The chapter began with Jethro coming from Midian and ends with his return there -- a self-contained narrative unit. The question of whether Jethro ever fully joined Israel is left unresolved; Num. 10:29-32 records Moses' later attempt to persuade Khovav (possibly Jethro or his son) to remain as a wilderness guide. EMET renders "sent away [vayeshallakh]...he went [vayelekh] for himself [lo] to his land [el-artso]." LOGOS renders "sent away...and he went to him, to his land." PNEUMA renders "sent his father-in-law on his way, and Jethro returned to his own land." The consensus renders "Moses sent his father-in-law away, and he went to his own land" -- following PNEUMA's phrasing of "his own land" for the ethical dative *lo* + *el-artso*, which captures the reflexive sense ("his own") without the awkward "to him" (LOGOS). The consensus omits "Jethro" in the second clause, following the MT which uses only pronouns. LXX: *exapesteilen Mouses ton gambron autou kai ocheto eis ten gen autou* ("Moses sent away his father-in-law, and he departed to his own land").

---

*Translated from the Leningrad Codex (B19A) with reference to Codex Vaticanus (LXX). All transliterations follow standard academic conventions. Hebrew text follows the Masoretic vocalization.*
